macro_events.event_summary: Iranian drones reportedly struck Amazon (AWS/logistics) facilities in the Middle East, marking a direct attack on US corporate infrastructure in the region.
primary_drivers: Direct operational/insurance hit to Amazon Middle East infrastructure
Escalation risk pricing into defense/cyber/missile-defense names

beneficiaries.rationale: Lockheed makes THAAD and PAC-3 missile interceptors — the exact systems used to defend Gulf US/allied assets from Iranian drone/missile attacks. Direct strike on US corporate infrastructure raises probability of expanded regional missile-defense FMS orders.

Raytheon makes Patriot and produces Coyote counter-drone interceptors actively deployed in CENTCOM. Drone attack on US corporate sites is the highest-probability catalyst for accelerated counter-UAS procurement.
Kratos is a pure-play unmanned systems and counter-drone target/training provider; small-cap leverage to any counter-UAS supplemental funding response.
AeroVironment's Switchblade loitering munitions and Puma ISR drones are the direct counter to Shahed-class threats; Pentagon counter-drone budgets typically expand after such incidents.
Brent risk premium widens on any Iran-linked kinetic event in the Gulf; Exxon has the largest US-major leverage to higher crude with low Iran-direct asset risk.

Palo Alto is the leading enterprise cybersecurity name; an attack on Amazon facilities (even kinetic) raises board-level scrutiny on cloud/critical-infrastructure security spend, especially against Iranian APT groups.

losers.rationale: Direct asset damage, AWS Middle East region (Bahrain/UAE) service risk, insurance/remediation costs, and customer concern over data sovereignty in the region. Even if financial impact is modest, headline risk dominates intraday.
Delta has significant Middle East routing exposure via JFK-DXB/TLV partnerships; airspace closures and rerouting around Gulf raise fuel burn just as oil spikes — double hit.
United operates the most US-carrier nonstops to the Gulf and India via Middle East airspace; rerouting cost and demand softness on Gulf routes.
Carnival's Middle East/Red Sea itineraries already disrupted by Houthi activity; an Iran-linked escalation extends cancellations and rerouting into peak booking window.
Google Cloud also operates Middle East regions (Doha, Dammam, Tel Aviv); peer-rotation risk as enterprises reassess hyperscaler regional concentration even though no direct strike.

headlines: Iranian drones strike Amazon sites in Middle East

macro_events.event_summary: Deutsche Post (DHL) at its AGM flagged ongoing tariff-related disruption to global trade flows but raised its dividend to €1.90 and reaffirmed its 2026 EBIT outlook.
primary_drivers: Global logistics read-through: a major freight carrier reaffirming guidance signals stable parcel/freight volumes despite tariffs
Tariff turmoil commentary reinforces ongoing cross-border trade friction theme
Dividend hike signals management confidence in cash flows

beneficiaries.rationale: Direct US-listed peer to Deutsche Post/DHL Express in international parcel and freight. DHL reaffirming 2026 EBIT despite tariff headwinds is a positive read-through for UPS international segment volumes and pricing.

FedEx competes directly with DHL in international express and air freight. Stable DHL guidance suggests cross-border volumes are holding up better than feared, supporting FDX's international priority margins.

Expeditors is a freight forwarder whose ocean/air volumes track the same global trade flows DHL reports on. A 'tariff turmoil but guidance intact' message implies forwarders are successfully repricing rather than losing volume.

losers.rationale: Forward Air is more domestic/LTL exposed; DHL's reaffirmation specifically benefits international peers and highlights that the freight strength is in cross-border express, not in segments where FWRD competes. Relative-rotation loser.

headlines: Deutsche Post AGM: CEO flags tariff turmoil, lifts dividend to €1.90 and backs 2026 EBIT view
